Name: adaptive_mixed_precision_quantization
Title: Adaptive Mixed-Precision Quantization for Efficient Kernel-Level Inference
Short Hypothesis: By implementing a kernel-level adaptive mixed-precision quantization framework, we can significantly enhance the inference efficiency of large language models (LLMs) on commodity GPUs while preserving model accuracy. This approach will dynamically allocate bit-widths based on real-time profiling of layer-wise performance metrics.
Related Work: Current mixed-precision quantization techniques often rely on static bit-width settings or heuristic methods that do not adapt to the actual runtime characteristics of the model. Existing works like SliM-LLM and KVmix offer improvements but do not incorporate real-time adaptive profiling. This proposal distinguishes itself by integrating kernel-level profiling to dynamically adjust precision during inference, leading to better performance and reduced memory usage without compromising accuracy.
Abstract: The growing demand for efficient inference in large language models (LLMs) necessitates innovative approaches to mixed-precision quantization that not only reduce computational overhead but also maintain accuracy. We propose a novel adaptive mixed-precision quantization framework that leverages kernel-level profiling to dynamically adjust bit-width allocations based on real-time performance metrics such as latency, memory bandwidth, and layer occupancy on commodity GPUs. Our approach seeks to optimize inference efficiency by allocating higher precision to critical layers while employing aggressive quantization on less impactful ones. We will conduct experiments on popular LLM architectures, evaluating the performance of our framework against state-of-the-art quantization methods. The expected outcome is a measurable improvement in inference speed and memory efficiency, with a clear focus on reproducibility and hardware-fidelity. This research aims to bridge the gap between theoretical advancements in mixed-precision quantization and practical deployment on real hardware, providing a framework that can be readily adopted by practitioners.
Experiments: Set up a baseline using standard mixed-precision quantization on various LLM architectures (e.g., Llama, GPT-3).
Implement the adaptive mixed-precision quantization framework, incorporating real-time profiling of layer performance metrics.
Evaluate the performance under varying workloads and contexts, measuring p50/p99 latency, throughput, and energy consumption.
Conduct A/B tests comparing the adaptive approach against static mixed-precision quantization and existing state-of-the-art methods.
Analyze the impact of adaptive quantization on model accuracy, memory usage, and overall inference speed.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the complexity of real-time profiling impacting overall throughput, the challenge of maintaining accuracy during aggressive quantization, and the need for extensive testing across various model architectures. Limitations may also arise from hardware-specific dependencies affecting generalizability.
